quiz.title: Cardiology: Heart Failure Management
quiz.description: Comprehensive review of acute and chronic heart failure management including pharmacotherapy, device therapy, and lifestyle modifications. Covers HFrEF, HFpEF, and acute decompensated heart failure.
quiz.subject: Cardiology

questions.question_text: A 32-year-old woman at 12 weeks gestation presents to the clinic for routine prenatal care. She has a history of hypertension and is currently taking lisinopril 10 mg daily. Her blood pressure today is 148/92 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue lisinopril and increase dose to 20 mg daily

questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors (like lisinopril) are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenic effects including renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios, and fetal death. They should be discontinued immediately upon pregnancy recognition. Labetalol is a safe first-line antihypertensive in pregnancy. Hydrochlorothiazide is generally avoided in pregnancy due to risk of volume depletion.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.question_text: A 68-year-old man with a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 30%) presents for follow-up. He is currently on metoprolol succinate, lisinopril, and spironolactone with good tolerance. His blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 68 bpm, and creatinine 1.1 mg/dL. Which of the following medications should be added to optimize guideline-directed medical therapy?
questions.explanation: Current guidelines recommend quadruple therapy for HFrEF: ACE inhibitor/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA), and SGLT2 inhibitor. This patient is on the first three pillars and should have an SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin) added. SGLT2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalizations regardless of diabetes status.
questions.subject: Cardiology
questions.topic: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction

questions.question_text: A 72-year-old woman with a history of heart failure presents to the ED with worsening dyspnea over 3 days. She has bilateral lower extremity edema and crackles halfway up both lung fields. Her weight has increased by 8 kg since her last visit 2 weeks ago. Vital signs: BP 142/88 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, RR 24/min, O2 sat 88% on room air. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
questions.explanation: This patient has acute decompensated heart failure with volume overload. IV loop diuretics (furosemide) are first-line therapy to achieve rapid diuresis. The IV route is preferred over oral in acute settings due to better bioavailability and faster onset. While nitroglycerin and NIPPV may be adjunctive therapies, diuresis is the primary intervention for volume overload.
questions.subject: Cardiology

questions.question_text: A 78-year-old woman with hypertension and diabetes presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion. Echocardiogram shows LVEF 58% with diastolic dysfunction and elevated filling pressures. BNP is 420 pg/mL. She has mild lower extremity edema. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacologic management?
questions.explanation: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) management focuses on treating underlying conditions (hypertension, diabetes) and symptom control with diuretics. Unlike HFrEF, there is limited evidence for mortality benefit from ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, or MRAs in HFpEF. SGLT2 inhibitors show promise but are not yet standard. Diuretics remain the mainstay for congestion management.
questions.subject: Cardiology
questions.topic: Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction

questions.question_text: A 55-year-old man with acute anterior STEMI complicated by cardiogenic shock is in the CCU. Despite IV fluids, his blood pressure remains 82/54 mmHg with cool extremities and altered mental status. Cardiac index is 1.8 L/min/m². Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock requires inotropic support to improve cardiac output. Dobutamine is a beta-1 agonist that increases contractility and cardiac output, making it first-line for cardiogenic shock. Norepinephrine is used for vasodilatory shock. Milrinone is a phosphodiesterase inhibitor reserved for refractory cases. Further fluid boluses would worsen pulmonary edema in this setting.
questions.subject: Cardiology
